content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5991          322-6441          322-6444


September 16, 1980


Ms. Lillie R. Watts
Legal Assistant
Tosco Corporation
10100 Santa Monica Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90067

Dear Ms. Watts:

     This is in response to your letter dated August 21,
1980, on behalf of Tosco Corporation. You have asked, for
the purpose of having the corporation comply with the lobby-
ist reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act,
whether the following agencies are state agencies within the
meaning of the Act:

     Kern County Air Pollution Control District
     San Joaquin Valley Basin Air Pollution Coordinating Council
     Bay Area Air Quality Management District
     California Regional Water Quality Control Board
     South Coast Air Quality Management District

     State agency is defined by Government Code Section
82049 to mean,

          ...(E)very state office, department, division,
          bureau, board and commission, and the Legislature,
          but does not include the courts or any agency in
          the judicial branch of government.

     The Commission, by regulation, has provided additional
guidance for determining on a case by case basis when an
agency is a state agency for the purposes of the Act. The
regulation provides that an agency is a state agency only if
it satisfies all the following criteria:

          (a) The agency is authorized by statute,
          executive order or the California Constitution.

Ms. Lillie R. Watts
September 16, 1980
Page Two


     (b) At least one voting member is an elected
state officer or is appointed by an elected state
officer or an agency official or a state agency.

     (c) The agency is financed in part by any
state funds or is subject to appropriation in the
state budget.

     (d) An area larger than one county is included
in its jurisdiction.

                              2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18249.

     The Kern County Air Pollution Control District, the
San Joaquin Valley Basin Air Pollution Coordinating Council,
and the Bay Area Air Quality Manatement District do not
satisfy all the criteria set forth above in the regulation.
None of these agencies, as required by the regulation, has a
voting member that is an elected state officer or a voting
member appointed by a state official or state agency. There-
fore, these agencies are not state agencies within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act and its lobbyist reporting
requirements.

     Of the agencies listed by you for our consideration,
only the status of the South Coast Air Quality Management
District as a state agency has previously been reviewed by
the Commission. Although the South Coast Air Quality Management
District satisfied the criteria established in Regulation
18249, it was determined by the Commission's staff that the
District was not a state agency, but rather a local agency
because of certain overriding factors.

     First, many air quality or air pollution districts are
limited in their jurisdiction to one county. Second, a
substantial part of the finances for air pollution control
districts come from local funds. Third, the Air Resources
Board is the true state agency in the air pollution control
area. The Board adopts regulations which supersede all
local regulations and must be complied with by local districts
including South Coast. Moreover, all variances and permits
granted by local districts must be approved by the Air Resources
Board.

     Finally, the California Regional Water Quality Control
Board satisfies all the criteria set forth in Regulation
18249 and for the purpose of Conflict of Interest Codes it

Ms. Lillie R. Watts
September 16, 1980
Page Three


is considered by the Commission to be a state agency. There-
fore, the Board is a state agency for the lobbyist reporting
requirements of Chapter 6 of the Act.

     I hope that this letter is helpful. If you have any
questions regarding this matter, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Dwight E. Dickerson
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DED:kp

Tosco Corporation
10100 SANTA MONICA BOULEVARD
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067
213/552-7000

August 21, 1980


Mr. John Greenwood
Technical Assistance/Administration Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
Sacramento, California     95814

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

For the purpose of complying with the reporting requirements under
the Political Reform Act of 1974, Tosco Corporation would like to
have your advice as to whether or not the following are considered
state agencies:

     South Coast Air Quality Management District
     Bay Area Water Quality Management
     Kern County Air Pollution Control District
     San Joaquin Valley Basin Air Pollution Coordinating Council
     California Regional Water Quality Control Board
     Bay Area Air Quality Management District

We would greatly appreciate receiving the aforesaid advice.

Very truly yours,

Lilie R. Watts
Legal Assistant

LRW:pb

You have asked, for the purpose of having the corporation comply with the lobbyist reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act, whether the following agencies are state agencies within the meaning of the Act: Kern County Air Pollution Control District San Joaquin Valley Basin Air Pollution Coordinating Council Bay Area Air Quality Management District California Regional Water Quality Control Board South Coast Air Quality Management District State agency is defined by Government Code Section 82049 to mean, ...(E)very state office, department, division, bureau, board and commission, and the Legislature, but does not include the courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government. The Commission, by regulation, has provided additional guidance for determining on a case by case basis when an agency is a state agency for the purposes of the Act. The regulation provides that an agency is a state agency only if it satisfies all the following criteria: (a) The agency is authorized by statute, executive order or the California Constitution. (b) At least one voting member is an elected state officer or is appointed by an elected state officer or an agency official or a state agency. (c) The agency is financed in part by any state funds or is subject to appropriation in the state budget. (d) An area larger than one county is included in its jurisdiction. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18249. None of these agencies, as required by the regulation, has a voting member that is an elected state officer or a voting member appointed by a state official or state agency.
